volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Merchant (Section 2.2.301 - Section 2.3.385)

sections.label: Section 2.2.301
sections.original: Sed ausimne ego tibi eloqui fideliter ?
sections.translation: But , if I wished at all , could I venture to disclose something to you ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.302
sections.original: Audacter .
sections.translation: Boldly .

sections.label: Section 2.2.302b
sections.original: Animum advorte .
sections.translation: Give heed , then .

sections.label: Section 2.2.302c
sections.original: Fiet sedulo .
sections.translation: It shall be carefully done .

sections.label: Section 2.2.303
sections.original: Hodie ire in ludum occepi litterarium ,
sections.translation: This day , Lysimachus , I ’ ve begun to go to school to learn my letters . I know three letters already .

sections.label: Section 2.2.304b
sections.original: Quid ternas ?
sections.translation: How ? Three letters ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.304c
sections.original: Amo .
sections.translation: A M O [ I am in love ].

sections.label: Section 2.2.305
sections.original: Tun capite cano amas , senex nequissime ?
sections.translation: What ! you , in love , with your hoary head , you most shocking old fellow ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.306
sections.original: Si canum seu istuc rutilum sive atrumst , amo .
sections.translation: Whether that is hoary , or whether red , or whether black , I ’ m in love .

sections.label: Section 2.2.307
sections.original: Ludificas nunc tu me hic , opinor , Demipho .
sections.translation: You ’ re now playing upon me in this , I fancy , Demipho .

sections.label: Section 2.2.308
sections.original: Decide collum stanti , si falsum loquor ;
sections.translation: Cut my throat , if it ’ s false , what I ’ m saying . That you may be sure I ’ m in love , take a knife , and do you cut off

sections.label: Section 2.2.310
sections.original: digitum vel aurem vel tu nasum vel labrum :
sections.translation: either my finger , or my ear , or my nose , or my lip : if I move me , or feel that I ’ m being cut , then , Lysimachus , I give you leave to torture me to death here with being in love .

sections.label: Section 2.2.313
sections.original: Si umquam vidistis pictum amatorem , em illic est .
sections.translation: If ever you ’ ve seen a lover in a picture , why , there he is ( pointing at DEMIPHO ) : for really , in my way of thinking , an antiquated , decrepit old man

sections.label: Section 2.2.315
sections.original: tantidemst quasi sit signum pictum in pariete .
sections.translation: is just about the same as though he were a figure painted upon a wall .

sections.label: Section 2.2.316
sections.original: Nunc tu me , credo , castigare cogitas .
sections.translation: Now , I suppose , you are thinking of censuring me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.317
sections.original: Egon té ?
sections.translation: What , I , censure you ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.317b
sections.original: Nihil est iam quod tu mihi suscenseas :
sections.translation: Well , there ’ s no reason that you should censure me . Other distinguished men have done the like before .

sections.label: Section 2.2.319_320
sections.original: humanum amarest , humanum autem ignoscerest :
sections.translation: It ’ s natural to be in love , it ’ s natural , as well , to be considerate . Then , please , don ’ t reprove me ; no inclination impelled me to this .

sections.label: Section 2.2.322
sections.original: Quin non obiurgo .
sections.translation: Why , I ’ m not reproving you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.322b
sections.original: At ne deteriorem tamen
sections.translation: But still , don ’ t you think any the worse of me for acting thus .

sections.label: Section 2.2.323b
sections.original: Egon te ? ah , ne di siverint .
sections.translation: I , think the worse of you ? O , may the Deities forbid it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.324
sections.original: Vide sís modo etiam .
sections.translation: Still , please , only do take care of that .

sections.label: Section 2.2.324b
sections.original: Visumst .
sections.translation: Due care is taken .

sections.label: Section 2.2.324c
sections.original: Certen ?
sections.translation: Quite sure ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.324d
sections.original: Perdis me .
sections.translation: You ’ re wearing me out .

sections.label: Section 2.2.325
sections.original: hic homo ex amore insanit . numquid vis ?
sections.translation: This person ’ s deranged through love . ( To DEMIPHO .) Do you desire aught with me ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.325b
sections.original: Vale .
sections.translation: Farewell !

sections.label: Section 2.2.326
sections.original: Ad portum propero , nam ibi mihi negotium est .
sections.translation: I ’ m making haste to the harbour ; for I ’ ve got business there .

sections.label: Section 2.2.327
sections.original: Bene ambulato .
sections.translation: Good luck go with you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.327b
sections.original: Bene vale .—
sections.translation: Heartily fare you well .

sections.label: Section 2.2.327c
sections.original: Bene sit tibi .
sections.translation: Kindly fare you well .

sections.label: Section 2.2.328
sections.original: quin mihi quoque etiamst ad portum negotium .
sections.translation: ( DEMIPHO continues , to himself .) And what ’ s more , I too as well have got some business at the harbour ; now , therefore , I shall be off thither . But , look ! most opportunely I see my son . I ’ ll wait for the fellow ; it ’ s necessary for me now to see him ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.331
sections.original: huic persuadere quo modo potis siem ,
sections.translation: to persuade him , as far as I possibly can , to sell her to me , and not make a present of her to his mother ; for I ’ ve heard that he has brought her as a present for her . But I have need of precaution , that he mayn ’ t any way imagine that I have set my fancy upon her .

sections.label: Section 2.3.335
sections.original: Homó me misérior nullúst aeque , opínor ,
sections.translation: Never , I do think , was any person more wretched than myself , nor one who had more everlasting crosses . Isn ’ t it the fact , that whatever thing there is that I have commenced to attempt , it cannot fall out to my wish according as I desire ? To such an extent is some evil fortune always befalling me ,

sections.label: Section 2.3.340
sections.original: bonum quaé meum comprimit cónsilium .
sections.translation: which overwhelms my fair intentions . To my misfortune , I procured me a mistress to please my inclination ; I acquired her for a sum of money , fancying that I could keep her unknown to my father . He has found her out , and has seen her , and has undone me . Nor have I yet determined what to say when he asks me ,

sections.label: Section 2.3.345
sections.original: ita animi decem in pectore incerti certant .
sections.translation: so much do uncertain thoughts , aye , tenfold , struggle within my breast ; nor know I now in my mind what resolution I can possibly take ; so much uncertainty , mingled with anxiety , is there in my feelings , at one moment the advice of my servant pleases me , then again it doesn ’ t please me , and it doesn ’ t seem possible for my father

sections.label: Section 2.3.350
sections.original: induci ut putet matri ancillam emptam esse illam .
sections.translation: to be induced to think that she was bought as a maid-servant for my mother . Now , if I say , as is the fact , and declare that I purchased her for myself , what will he think of me ? He may take her away , too , and carry her hence beyond sea , to be sold !

sections.label: Section 2.3.354_355
sections.original: scio saevos quam sit , domo doctus . igitur
sections.translation: Well taught at home , I know how severe he is . Is this , then , being in love ? I ’ d rather be at the plough-tail [*] than love in this fashion . Before to-day , long ago , he drove me away against my inclination from his house , my home , and bade me go and traffic . There did I meet with this misfortune . When its misery can surpass its pleasure , what is there delightful in it ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.360
sections.original: nequíquam abdidi , abscondidi , abstrusam habebam :
sections.translation: In vain I ’ ve hidden her , concealed her , kept her in secret ; my father ’ s a very fly [*] ; nothing can be kept away from him ; nothing so sacred or so profane is there , but that he ’ s there at once ; neither have I any assured hope in my mind through which to feel confidence in my fortunes .

sections.label: Section 2.3.364
sections.original: Quíd illuc est quod solus secum fabulatur filius ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the reason of this , that my son is talking to himself alone ? He seems to me anxious about some matter , I know not what .

sections.label: Section 2.3.365b
sections.original: Attatae ,
sections.translation: Heyday , now ! Why , surely it ’ s my father here that I see . I ’ ll go and accost him . ( Accosting him .) How goes it , father ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.367
sections.original: Vnde incedis , quid festinas , gnate mi ?
sections.translation: Whence do you come ? Why are you in a hurry , my son ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.367b
sections.original: Recte , pater .
sections.translation: It ’ s all right , father .

sections.label: Section 2.3.368
sections.original: Ita volo , sed istúc quid est , tibi quod commutatust color ?
sections.translation: So I trust ; but what ’ s the reason that your colour ’ s so changed ? Do you feel ill at all ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.369b
sections.original: Nescio quid meo animost aegre , pater .
sections.translation: I know not what it is affects my spirits , father ;

sections.label: Section 2.3.370
sections.original: poste hac nocte non quievi satis mea ex sententia .
sections.translation: this last night I didn ’ t rest quite as well as I wished .

sections.label: Section 2.3.371
sections.original: Per mare ut vectu's (vectus es) , nunc oculi terram mirantur tui .
sections.translation: As you ’ ve been travelling by sea , your eyes , I suppose , are at present rather unaccustomed to the shore .

sections.label: Section 2.3.372
sections.original: Magis opinor —
sections.translation: No doubt it is that ; but it will be going off presently .

sections.label: Section 2.3.372b
sections.original: Id est profecto ; verum actutum abscesserit .
sections.translation: Troth , it ’ s for that reason you are pale ; if you were prudent , you ’ d go home and lie down .

sections.label: Section 2.3.374
sections.original: Otium non est : mandatis rebus praevorti volo .
sections.translation: I haven ’ t the leisure ; I wish to attend to business on commission .

sections.label: Section 2.3.375
sections.original: Cras agito , perendie agito .
sections.translation: Attend to it to-morrow ; the day after , attend to it .

sections.label: Section 2.3.375b
sections.original: Saepe ex te audivi , pater :
sections.translation: I ’ ve often heard from you , father , it behoves all wise men , the first thing , to give their earliest attention to business upon commission .

sections.label: Section 2.3.377
sections.original: Age igitur ; nolo advorsari tuam advorsum sententiam .
sections.translation: Do so , then ; I have no wish to be striving against your opinion .

sections.label: Section 2.3.378
sections.original: Salvos sum , siquidem ísti dicto solida et perpetuast fides .
sections.translation: I ’ m all right , if , indeed , his adherence to that sentiment is immoveable and lasting .

sections.label: Section 2.3.379
sections.original: Quid illuc est quod ille á me solus se in consilium sevocat ?
sections.translation: Why is it that he calls himself aside into counsel with himself ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.380
sections.original: iam nón vereor ne illam me amare hic potuerit resciscere ;
sections.translation: I ’ m not afraid now lest he should be able to come to know that I ’ m in love with her , because I ’ ve not as yet done anything in a foolish manner , as people in love are wont to do .

sections.label: Section 2.3.382
sections.original: Res adhuc quidem hercle in tutost , nam hunc nescire sat scio
sections.translation: I ’ faith , the affair for the present is really quite safe ; for I ’ m quite certain that he doesn ’ t know anything about that mistress of mine ; if he did know , his talk would have been different .

sections.label: Section 2.3.384
sections.original: Quin ego hunc adgredior de illa ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t I accost him about her ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.384b
sections.original: Quin ego hinc me amolior ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t I betake myself off hence ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.385
sections.original: eo ego , ut quae mandata amicus amicis tradam .
sections.translation: ( Aloud .) I ’ m going to deliver the commissions from my friends to their friends .
